request.question: In between J Puttanna Kanagal and John Cassavetes, who acted in many Hollywood films, notably "Rosemary's Baby"?

title: Puttanna Kanagal Award
Kanagal, Mysore
Masanada Hoovu
Vijaya Bhaskar
Kappu Bilupu
P. H. Vishwanath
Puttanna Kanagal
Shivaram
Runamukthalu
John Cassavetes
sentences: The following is the list of Award winners for the Karnataka State Puttanna Kanagal award.
Kanagal was among the front runners in Kannada cinema's most successful film directors.
In his memory and honor, this award is presented to the directors every year during the Karnataka State Awards function.
Kanagal is a village in Mysore district, Karnataka.
It is in periyapatna taluk 30 km from Taluk place.
It is very close to Coorg and Hassan district.
It is located on the bank of the Cauvery River.
It is also birthplace of legendary Kannada film director Puttanna Kanagal, and his brother, lyricist Kanagal Prabhakara Shasthry.
Villagers are mainly farmers though many of the younger generation are working in other fields such as medical, engineering, teaching, police, army, and industries.
Masanada Hoovu (Kannada: ಮಸಣದ ಹೂವು ) ("Graveyard Flower") is a 1984 Indian Kannada language film directed by Puttanna Kanagal, based on T. R. Subba Rao's novel of the same name.
It stars Jayanthi, Ambarish and Aparna in lead roles.
It was Puttanna Kanagal's last as a director.
His friend, director K. S. L. Swamy, completed the film.
"Masanada Hoove" won multiple awards at the 1985-86 Karnataka State Film Awards including Best Actress (Jayanthi) and Best Supporting Actor.
Vijaya Bhaskar (; 1924–2002) was an Indian music director and composer who composed music for several mainstream and experimental feature films in the Kannada film industry.
Scoring music for over 600 feature films, Bhaskar worked in Tamil, Telugu, Malayalam, Marathi, Tulu and Konkani language films as well.
He was a part of all of popular Kannada film director KSL Swamy's (Ravi) movies and was also known for his association with acclaimed directors Puttanna Kanagal and Adoor Gopalakrishnan.
Kappu Bilupu (Kannada: ಕಪ್ಪು ಬಿಳುಪು, which means Black and White ) is a 1969 Indian Kannada language film directed by Puttanna Kanagal.
Based on a novel of the same name by Aryambha Pattabhi, this movie revolves around the contrasting behaviors of an identical, yet distinct pair of twins.
The film starred Kalpana in dual roles.
Puttanna himself remade the movie in Tamil as "Irulum Oliyum" and in Telugu as "Iddaru Ammayilu".
P. H. Vishwanath (Kannada: ಪಿ.
ಎಚ್.
ವಿಶ್ವನಾಥ್ ) is an Indian film director, writer and a producer who works in Kannada cinema.
Apart from films, Vishwanath has directed several documentaries and tele-series.
He started his film career as an assistant director to Puttanna Kanagal with "Dharmasere" (1979) being his first release.
Following this, he assisted for many films directed by Kanagal, Joe Simon, Hunsur Krishnamurthy and T. S. Nagabharana.

Puttanna Kanagal (born Shubraveshti Ramaswamiah Seetharama Sharma, 1 December 1933 – 5 June 1985), fondly called the 'Chitra Bramha' (God of Films, Kannada) was an Indian filmmaker and is considered as one of Kannada's cinema's greatest filmmakers.
S. Shivaram (born 1938), popularly known simply as Shivaram or Shivaramanna, is an Indian actor, producer and director whose Kannada cinema career has spanned six decades.
He has played roles including lead hero performances, character roles, comedic roles, as well as supporting parts.
He teamed up with his elder brother, S. Ramanathan and produced several films under the name Rashi Brothers apart from directing "Hrudaya Sangama" in 1972.
The brothers together produced few Bollywood films as well.
As an actor, Shivaram has the distinction of working with many stalwart directors out of which his association with Puttanna Kanagal is much remembered.
Runamukthalu (Kannada: ಋಣ ಮುಕ್ತಳು ) is a 1984 Indian Kannada language film directed and co-produced by Puttanna Kanagal starring Bharathi, Padmavasanthi and Ramakrishna.
The film is based on the novel "Runa" written by Anupama Niranjana.
The film was produced under the banner "Kanagal Creations".
John Nicholas Cassavetes ( ; December 9, 1929 – February 3, 1989) was a Greek-American actor, film director, and screenwriter.

Cassavetes was a pioneer of American independent film, writing and directing over a dozen movies, which he partially self-financed, and pioneered the use of improvisation and a realistic cinéma vérité style.
He also acted in many Hollywood films, notably "Rosemary's Baby" (1968) and "The Dirty Dozen" (1967).
He studied acting with Don Richardson, using an acting technique based on muscle memory.
His income from acting made it possible for him to direct his own films independently.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Role: This agent summarizes first-hop evidence and exposes the bridge entity, any missing fact(s), ambiguity, and concrete next-hop retrieval directions. Do not attempt to produce the final answer—the summary should tell the next agent exactly what to fetch to finish the multi-hop chain.

Output requirements (single `summary` value only):
- Start the summary with the header: First-hop findings:
- Include these labeled parts (use a short bullet list or 2–4 concise sentences; keep overall output brief):
  1) Evidence found: For each person/entity named in the question, list which passage(s) mention matching candidate pages/titles and quote any explicit fact tokens found there (names, birth years, publication years, short parenthetical notes). Keep quoted fragments <=15 words and cite the passage title/label for each fact.
  2) Ambiguities: If a name yields multiple candidate pages or variants in the passages, explicitly enumerate those candidate titles/labels as they appear; if an expected page/variant is missing, note that too.
  3) Bridge entity / Missing fact: Explicitly name the bridge entity exactly as it appears in the passages (the person, work, or organization that links to the next hop) and state the precise missing fact needed to answer the question (e.g., a birth date, founding year, canonical series ordering).
  4) Retrieval direction: Provide 2–4 concrete, prioritized next-hop targets (specific page titles or disambiguation pages to fetch, and/or exact sections to check such as lede/infobox/Career/History). Include short example search keywords or one-line instructions (e.g., "open '<Name>' page and check lede/infobox and 'Career' section for 'Italian champion' year").
- End with a brief explicit note that the final answer is deferred until those pages are retrieved.

Hard constraints (do not violate):
- Do not provide a final comparison or answer here. Do not guess or invent facts not present in the provided passages or introduce outside knowledge.
- Do not assume which candidate is intended when multiple candidates exist.
- Avoid long verbatim copying of passages (>15 words).
- Preserve concise factual phrasing and verbatim tokens that appear in the passages (names, dates, page titles) when reporting evidence.

Keep the summary concise and focused—just the first-hop findings needed to guide the next retrieval hop.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Output contract:
- Produce exactly one field named `query` whose value is a single short retrieval query string (one line, no surrounding explanation or extra fields).
- Do not answer, summarize, or explain anything in `query`. Do not include fallback instructions or user-facing requests for more info in this field.

Construction rules (apply the most specific rule that fits the inputs):
1) Linking two named works: If `question` or `summary_1` names two or more specific works (songs, tracks, titles) and the missing evidence is the bridge between them, emit a concise interrogative that names both works and requests the linking evidence (artist / album / recording). Keep it short (about ≤ 12 words). Example form: "Which artist or album recorded both 'Work A' and 'Work B'?"
2) Missing album artist/genre: If `summary_1` indicates the album title is present but the artist or genre is unknown, emit a compact search string of the form: <AlbumTitle> album artist genre
   - No punctuation, no question phrasing, no extra words. Example: "Levitate album artist genre"
3) Sports season-year lookups: If the `question` contains a specific season-year token and the missing bridge is a league-season page, emit a short query that includes the year token, a league identifier (e.g., "NHL" or "National Hockey League"), and a cue such as "regular season" or "season count". Keep it concise (≤ ~10 tokens).
4) Default: Otherwise, produce one concise search-style query (≤ ~12 words) that uses visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` targeted at retrieving the missing supporting evidence (artist, album, recording, genre, season page, etc.).

General guidance:
- Keep queries compact; avoid lists, long page titles, or copying large spans from hidden targets.
- Do not invent facts or candidate answers; use only visible tokens or neutral retrieval cues.
- Prefer a short search string; use an interrogative only for the two-works linking case in rule 1.
- The goal is to maximize recovery of the missing bridge evidence for the downstream retriever.

Return only the single `query` field.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Primary function: integrate the newly retrieved `passages` (second-hop evidence) with the provided `context` (first-hop) so the output is a grounded post-retrieval state that preserves the facts the final-answer generator needs. Resolve the earlier bridge when the supplied passages support doing so; retain uncertainty only when evidence is insufficient.

Behavior rules (minimal, general):
1) Preserve concise-answer behavior. If `context` already contains a concise direct answer (a short sentence or phrase), keep that text exactly as the first sentence of `summary`, unless it contradicts the `passages`.
2) When `passages` supply additional, directly supported facts about entities named in the `question` or `context`, append up to one brief supporting sentence per distinct named entity (keep total `summary` to 1–3 sentences, preferably 1–2). Each supporting sentence should: start with the entity's full name as shown in the passages, include a short paraphrased attribution drawn only from the passages (no more than ~10–12 words) such as nationality, a notable achievement, a birth date, or a current affiliation, and avoid inventing or inferring facts not present in the inputs.
3) When an object/entity page (for example, a car page, group page, or similar) lists associated people and a separate passage provides a biography for one of those listed people, produce a 1–2 sentence integrated summary: (a) first sentence: name the people as listed on the object page (e.g., "driven by X and Y" or "member of Group Z"); (b) optional second short clause/sentence: paraphrase key biographical facts from the person's passage (only facts present there — e.g., nationality, notable wins/awards, birth date, current team/affiliation). Keep wording compact and factual.
4) Ground everything in the provided `passages` and `context`. Do not add new claims, dates, roles, or affiliations not explicitly supported. Avoid long verbatim quotations from the passages; you may paraphrase short phrases (≤10 words) only if they appear in the sources, and you may copy short strings such as names or dates exactly as shown.
5) If the passages do not provide enough evidence to resolve the bridge, state that the passages do not specify the requested fact while still listing any supported names or facts. Do not guess or fabricate missing information.
6) Output contract: return only the field `summary` containing 1–3 concise sentences (preferably 1–2) that integrate the first-hop `context` and second-hop `passages` according to the rules above. Do not add commentary, answer-labels, or extraneous text.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2`, produce the field `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only the visible summaries (summary_1 and summary_2) together with the question as your evidence. Do not introduce external facts, sources, or claims not present in those summaries.
- Treat summary_2 (the later, integrated post-retrieval state) as primary evidence while still considering summary_1. Do not assume the final state is unresolved simply because summary_1 describes an earlier unresolved bridge; rely on the combined visible summaries to reach a conclusion.
- If summary_1 and summary_2 both clearly and unambiguously support the same binary (yes/no) conclusion to the question, then the value of the `answer` field must be exactly one lowercase token: either "yes" (if both summaries affirm the fact) or "no" (if both summaries deny the fact). The `answer` field must contain only that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no capitalization, no explanation, no additional words).
- If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, or do not together provide a clear binary yes/no verdict, produce a concise answer string in the `answer` field that best reflects what the summaries support. This concise answer may be a short phrase or sentence, but must still be supported solely by the summaries and must avoid adding new facts.
- Always minimize verbosity: prefer the single-token binary response when the summaries unambiguously agree; otherwise keep the answer brief and directly tied to the visible summaries.

Produce only the `answer` field as output.

response.hop1_query: In between J Puttanna Kanagal and John Cassavetes, who acted in many Hollywood films, notably "Rosemary's Baby"?
hop1_docs: John Cassavetes | John Nicholas Cassavetes ( ; December 9, 1929 – February 3, 1989) was a Greek-American actor, film director, and screenwriter.  Cassavetes was a pioneer of American independent film, writing and directing over a dozen movies, which he partially self-financed, and pioneered the use of improvisation and a realistic cinéma vérité style.  He also acted in many Hollywood films, notably "Rosemary's Baby" (1968) and "The Dirty Dozen" (1967).  He studied acting with Don Richardson, using an acting technique based on muscle memory.  His income from acting made it possible for him to direct his own films independently.

Rosemary's Baby (film) | Rosemary's Baby is a 1968 American psychological horror film written and directed by Roman Polanski, based on the bestselling 1967 novel of the same name by Ira Levin.  The cast features Mia Farrow, John Cassavetes, Ruth Gordon, Sidney Blackmer, Maurice Evans, Ralph Bellamy, Angela Dorian, Clay Tanner, and, in his feature film debut, Charles Grodin.  The film chronicles the story of a pregnant woman who suspects that an evil cult wants to take her baby for use in their rituals.

Puttanna Kanagal Award | The following is the list of Award winners for the Karnataka State Puttanna Kanagal award.  Kanagal was among the front runners in Kannada cinema's most successful film directors.  In his memory and honor, this award is presented to the directors every year during the Karnataka State Awards function.

P. H. Vishwanath | P. H. Vishwanath (Kannada: ಪಿ.  ಎಚ್.  ವಿಶ್ವನಾಥ್ ) is an Indian film director, writer and a producer who works in Kannada cinema.  Apart from films, Vishwanath has directed several documentaries and tele-series.  He started his film career as an assistant director to Puttanna Kanagal with "Dharmasere" (1979) being his first release.  Following this, he assisted for many films directed by Kanagal, Joe Simon, Hunsur Krishnamurthy and T. S. Nagabharana.

Kanagal, Mysore | Kanagal is a village in Mysore district, Karnataka.  It is in periyapatna taluk 30 km from Taluk place.  It is very close to Coorg and Hassan district.  It is located on the bank of the Cauvery River.  It is also birthplace of legendary Kannada film director Puttanna Kanagal, and his brother, lyricist Kanagal Prabhakara Shasthry.  Villagers are mainly farmers though many of the younger generation are working in other fields such as medical, engineering, teaching, police, army, and industries.

Masanada Hoovu | Masanada Hoovu (Kannada: ಮಸಣದ ಹೂವು ) ("Graveyard Flower") is a 1984 Indian Kannada language film directed by Puttanna Kanagal, based on T. R. Subba Rao's novel of the same name.  It stars Jayanthi, Ambarish and Aparna in lead roles.  It was Puttanna Kanagal's last as a director.  His friend, director K. S. L. Swamy, completed the film.  "Masanada Hoove" won multiple awards at the 1985-86 Karnataka State Film Awards including Best Actress (Jayanthi) and Best Supporting Actor.

  - "John Cassavetes" appears and is described as having "also acted in many Hollywood films, notably 'Rosemary's Baby' (1968)" (passage [1]); Rosemary's Baby cast also lists "John Cassavetes" (passage [2]).
- Ambiguities:
  - The question uses the variant "J Puttanna Kanagal" while the passages show "Puttanna Kanagal" (passage [5]) — possible naming/variant mismatch.
  - No passage here shows a list or sequence that places an intervening entry between those two names; the actual "in between" candidate is missing from the provided passages.
- Bridge entity / Missing fact:
  - Bridge entity present in passages: "John Cassavetes" (exactly as in passage [1]).
  - Precise missing fact needed: the name (exact page/title) of the entry that appears between "J Puttanna Kanagal" and "John Cassavetes" on the source/list being queried (i.e., the identity of the intervening person).
- Retrieval direction (prioritized):
  1) Open "Puttanna Kanagal" page (lede/infobox) to confirm the exact article title and any "J" variant (check lede/infobox/also-known-as).
     - Search example: Puttanna Kanagal page; check lede and infobox.
  2) Open "John Cassavetes" page (lede/intro) to re-confirm the quoted acting credit ("also acted... 'Rosemary's Baby'") and canonical article title (passage [1]).
     - Check lede/filmography section.
  3) Locate the source list/page where the two names are adjacent (this is the page the question references). Search Wikipedia for pages containing both names or for an alphabetical index/list used by the quiz:
     - Search keywords: "site:wikipedia.org \"Puttanna Kanagal\" \"John Cassavetes\" \"List\""
     - Alternatively fetch candidate lists/categories (e.g., any alphabetical index, "List of people", or category pages) and open the segment or page where entries starting with "J" are listed; then inspect the entry immediately between the two names.
  4) If the source is a single multi-name page (e.g., an index or category listing), open that page and check the exact entry between "J Puttanna Kanagal" and "John Cassavetes" (look at the neighborhood, lede lines, or link target).
Final answer is deferred until the above pages (especially the source list that orders these names) are retrieved.

Puttanna Kanagal is an Indian filmmaker (born Shubraveshti Ramaswamiah Seetharama Sharma; 1 December 1933 – 5 June 1985). John Cassavetes acted in many Hollywood films, notably Rosemary's Baby (1968).
